title: Incompleteness is necessary for activation of nonlocality without entanglement
authors: Atanu Bhunia, Saronath Halder, Preeti Parashar, Ritabrata Sengupta
abstract: A set of orthogonal product states is said to exhibit "quantum nonlocality without entanglement" if it is locally indistinguishable, i.e. no sequence of local operations and classical communication (LOCC) can perfectly discriminate the states. Building on this foundational idea, recent studies have highlighted the phenomenon of "genuine activation of hidden nonlocality", where a set of initially distinguishable orthogonal states becomes locally indistinguishable through orthogonality-preserving LOCC transformations. In this letter, we establish that any complete orthogonal product basis that is initially locally distinguishable remains so under all orthogonality-preserving local projective measurements, thereby ruling out activation via orthogonality-preserving local projective measurements and classical communication. We further introduce and formalise the notions of "strongly local sets", namely locally distinguishable sets that remain non-activable under all bipartitions. Interestingly, the study of "local activability" of distinguishable sets is useful to characterise the boundary between LOCC distinguishability and its irreversible loss in multipartite systems. Our results provide a rigorous structural understanding of local-to-nonlocal transitions in quantum state discrimination.
summary: This theoretical paper rigorously analyzes the concept of 'quantum nonlocality without entanglement' by studying how sets of orthogonal product states behave under local measurements. The core finding is that the structural completeness of the state set dictates its robustness: complete bases resist local indistinguishability, whereas incomplete bases can undergo 'activation' to become locally indistinguishable.
main_problem: The paper investigates the conditions under which quantum nonlocality can be activated—transforming a locally distinguishable set of states into a locally indistinguishable one—without relying on entanglement.
main_result: Completeness of an orthogonal product basis prevents activation via local projective measurements, while incompleteness allows for such activation, providing a structural understanding of local-to-nonlocal transitions.
method: The authors use rigorous mathematical proofs based on analyzing the constraints imposed on local measurement operators (LOCC/LPCC) when distinguishing or failing to distinguish specific sets of orthogonal product states.
model_or_system: The study focuses on orthogonal product states in composite quantum systems, particularly in bipartite and multipartite Hilbert spaces, analyzing the behavior of these states under local operations.
key_observables: Local distinguishability, local indistinguishability, and the structural property of 'completeness' of the product basis.
important_parameters: The dimensionality of the local Hilbert spaces (e.g., $\mathbb{C}^3 \otimes \mathbb{C}^3$ or $\mathbb{C}^3 \otimes \mathbb{C}^6$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often restricts itself to local projective measurements (LPCC) rather than general POVMs, and the theorems rely on the initial distinguishability of the state set.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the structural difference between complete (Fig. 2) and incomplete (Fig. 3) product bases regarding how post-measurement states are constrained, and show specific state representations in $\mathbb{C}^3 \otimes \mathbb{C}^3$ (Fig. 4).
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the general framework of local distinguishability and activation. It then proves that complete bases are robust against activation (Theorems 7, 8, 9). Subsequently, it contrasts this by analyzing specific incomplete sets ($S_2$) that *are* activable, concluding that 'incompleteness' is necessary for activation.
why_interesting: This work provides fundamental structural theorems about the boundaries of quantum information processing, specifically characterizing when local measurements can destroy the ability to distinguish quantum states, which has implications for quantum cryptography and resource theories.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Diagram to illustrate the statement of the problem.

FIG. 2: Tiling diagram for the states in S1. The red colored re- gion indicates the support of Alice’s measurement outcome KA 1 corresponding to the measurement set up KA ≡   KA 1 := P[|0⟩A], KA 2 := I −KA 1 }, resulting in post-measurement states which are all contained in the original set. The horizontal side of the tiles belongs to (A)lice and the vertical side of the tiles belongs to (B)ob.

FIG. 3: Tiling diagram for the states in S2. The outlined region indicates the support of Bob’s measurement outcome KB 1 , resulting in post-measurement states not all of which are contained in the original set. The horizontal side of the tiles belongs to (B)ob and the vertical side of the tiles belongs to (A)lice.
FIG. 4: Representation of product states in C3 ⊗C3. Tile indices correspond to consecutively ordered basis states of set S1, while tile colors indicate compatible measurement setups for both parties.

FIG. 5: Tiling diagram for the states in S2. The outlined region indicates the support of Bob’s measurement outcomes KB 1 and KB 2 resulting in post-measurement states are all contained in a UPB sub- space.
